content:
2 min read
1 hour ago

--

I’ve been building the ecommerce stack for my startup, and so far I’ve leaned on Claude Code to do most of the heavy lifting. For the most part it’s been great. As someone new to building web applications from the ground up, I’ve found it has sped up my workflow enormously. However, I have been following along as it went to understand what it’s been doing.
Recently, it made its first real mistake.
During deployment, I was migrating my products from a local database to an AWS Postgres database. Claude Code reported that it had lost 6 of my products. Luckily, I’d kept a spreadsheet as a backup, so I was able to recover them. But there was a second problem: for the remaining 14 products, it had used the wrong names and descriptions, and even formatted the images incorrectly. I fixed that too, but with no data pipeline in place, it meant hours of re-entering everything by hand.
That experience taught me something important. As capable and independent as Claude Code can be at solving complex problems, engineers still need to stay engaged and understand what the tool is doing. Context is everything.
To make sure this doesn’t happen again, I now keep a backup of my data before running anything risky. And honestly, I’m relieved this happened during a product migration rather than while building out a large, critical feature.
The hours I spent fixing it weren’t wasted. They taught me that developers aren’t going anywhere just yet. Our roles have just changed from doing the implementation to monitoring and instructing the work AI does for us.
